turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion this morning in case 22-148, Jack Daniels Properties v. VIP Products.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: This case is about a squeaky chewable dog toy that a dog toy company called Silly Squeakers designed to look like a bottle of Jack Daniels Whiskey. It doesn't entirely look like a bottle of Jack Daniels Whiskey. On the toy, the words Jack Daniels become Bad Spaniels and the phrase Old No. 7 Brand Tennessee Sour Mash Whiskey turns into the The Old No. 2 On Your Tennessee Carpet. The toy didn't really impress Jack Daniels, the liquor company. It owns trademarks in the distinctively shaped Jack Daniel's bottle and in many of the words and graphics on its label. As Jack Daniel's saw the toy infringed those trademarks by leading consumers to believe the Jack Daniel's was itself responsible for the toy. So Jack Daniel's brought an infringement suit. Now in a typical case of that kind the key question is whether the defendant's use of a trademark is likely to cause confusion about who made the product. So here the question would be whether consumers would think that Jack Daniel's had started making dog toys rather than that a dog toy company was spoofing Jack Daniel's. But this case never got to that likelihood of confusion question. That was because the Court below held that the First Amendment demands application of a rigorous threshold test whenever the product that's alleged to infringe is an expressive work. The Court thought that this toy was an expressive work because it parodies Jack Daniel's. So the Court applied that threshold test and threw out Jack Daniel's suit. The main question today is whether the Court was right to apply that very hard to meet First Amendment test most Courts call it the Rogers test in the circumstances here, essentially it's whether there needs to be some extra layer of First Amendment protection before Jack Daniel's can try to show that consumers will be confused by the Bad Spaniels toy. Our opinion today is narrow. We don't decide whether a special First Amendment test like Rogers is ever appropriate. We just say it's not appropriate here because Silly Squeakers, that's the dog toy company, by its own admission was using the Jack Daniel's trademark as a trademark. In other words, it was using those trademarks to identify the source of it's own product in the same way that a Nike Swoosh says to a customer buy this sneaker, it's Nike. The trademarks here were used to say buy this toy, it's a Bad Spaniels. Before this case, Rogers hadn't ever been applied when the alleged infringer had used the cribbed trademarks in that way. Instead, it had been applied when a trademark had been used for some other expressive function. So here is an example. A band was sued for using the trademark Barbie in their song Barbie Girl, you may have heard of that song. I'm a blond bimbo girl, in a fantasy world. Life in plastic, it's fantastic. Well here is another example, a character in the movie Hangover II walks around with a Louis Vuitton suitcase with the famous LV logo all the time calling it a Luis Vuitton. When Louis Vuitton brought suit as when Mateo the Barbie manufacturer brought suit, the Courts have said that a special First Amendment test should be applied. But until this case, no Court had ever used that test when a company cribbed a trademark for the purpose of identifying its own goods for sale and we hold that that was right. Whatever you think about using a special First Amendment test for the Barbie case or the Hangover II case, and we don't express any view on that subject. The test shouldn't have applied here and that so even though the Bad Spaniels toy might well be expressive in that its spoofs are parities a well-known American brand. Even if that's true, it's also true that the dog toy company was using the cribbed trademarks to identify its own Bad Spaniels toys for sale. It was using the marks as marks and when that so, there is a higher likelihood of consumer confusion about who made a product. Trademark Law is all about preventing that kind of confusion and in serving that role, Trademark Law is not in conflict with the First Amendment. So where marks are used to identify the maker of a product, no special First Amendment test should apply. Of course, that doesn't mean this toy really is confusing. The Courts below have to work that out and in doing so, it's perfectly okay to take into account Silly Squeakers' argument that this product is a parity that it mocks Jack Daniel's and so it's safe to say Jack Daniel's didn't make it. But that's an issue for the Courts below. The opinion is unanimous. Justice Sotomayor has filed a concurring opinion, in which Justice Alito joins. Justice Gorsuch has filed a concurring opinion, in which Justice Thomas and Justice Barrett join.
